verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: ॐ उशन् ह वै वाजश्रवसः सर्ववेदसं ददौ । तस्य ह नचिकेता नाम पुत्र आस ॥ १॥
verses.iast: oṃ uśan ha vai vājaśravasaḥ sarvavedasaṃ dadau । tasya ha naciketā nāma putra āsa ॥ 1 ॥
verses.trans: Desiring, Vajasravasa gave away all his wealth. He had a son named Nachiketa.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Symbolizes the seeker's need to renounce material attachment.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Pious ritual act of a devotee.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Father's offering as a test of faith.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Lower mind performing acts, giving birth to higher questioning.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: तं ह कुमारं सन्तं दक्षिणासु नीयमानासु श्रद्धाऽऽविवेश सोऽमन्यत ॥ २॥
verses.iast: taṃ ha kumāraṃ santaṃ dakṣiṇāsu nīyamānāsu śraddhā'viveśa so'manyata ॥ 2 ॥
verses.trans: As the gifts were being led away, faith entered the heart of the boy, Nachiketa, and he thought:

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shraddha is firm conviction in scriptural truth.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Unwavering devotion to the Supreme.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: God-given capacity to discriminate value.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: पीतोदका जग्धतृणा दुग्धदोहा निरिन्द्रियाः । अनन्दा नाम ते लोकास्तान् स गच्छति ता ददत् ॥ ३॥
verses.iast: pītodakā jagdhatṛṇā dugdhadohā nirindriyāḥ । anandā nāma te lokāstān sa gacchati tā dadat ॥ 3 ॥
verses.trans: Joyless are the worlds to which a man goes who offers gifts of barren cows.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Critique of selfish, ritualistic karma.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Insincere offerings block Divine grace.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Hypocritical giving leads to lower planes.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Mechanical ritual is a 'joyless world' of the ego.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: स होवाच पितरं तत कस्मै मां दास्यसीति द्वितीयं तृतीयं तं होवाच मृत्यवे त्वा ददामीति ॥ ४॥
verses.iast: sa hovāca pitaraṃ tata kasmai māṃ dāsyasīti dvitīyaṃ tṛtīyaṃ taṃ hovāca mṛtyave tvā dadāmīti ॥ 4 ॥
verses.trans: He asked his father, 'To whom will you give me?' His father replied in anger, 'To Death, I give you!'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Anger acts as a catalyst for liberation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Divine play leading the seeker to truth.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Test of the son's courage and dedication.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Threshold where the ego dies to transition to Truth.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: बहूनामेमि प्रथमो बहूनामेमि मध्यमः । किं स्विद्यमस्य कर्तव्यं यन्मयाद्य करिष्यति ॥ ५॥
verses.iast: bahūnāmemi prathamo bahūnāmemi madhyamaḥ । kiṃ svidyamasya kartavyaṃ yanmayādya kariṣyati ॥ 5 ॥
verses.trans: Nachiketa thought, 'Among many I go as the first; what has Yama to accomplish through me?'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Self-reflection of a qualified student.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Focus on the role of the disciple.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Recognition of a specific mission assigned by the Lord.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Soul's realization of its unique destiny.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: अनुपश्य यथा पूर्वे प्रतिपश्य तथाऽपरे । सस्यमिव मर्त्यः पच्यते सस्यमिवाजायते पुनः ॥ ६॥
verses.iast: anupaśya yathā pūrve pratipaśya tathā'pare । sasyamiva martyaḥ pacyate sasyamivājāyate punaḥ ॥ 6 ॥
verses.trans: Like corn, a mortal ripens and falls; like corn, he is born again.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Meditation on the cycle of Samsara.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Soul's movement under Divine governance.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Karmic law of repeated cycles.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: वैश्वानरः प्रविशत्यतिथिर्ब्राह्मणो गृहान् । तस्यैतां शान्तिं कुर्वन्ति हर वैवस्वतोदकम् ॥ ७॥
verses.iast: vaiśvānaraḥ praviśatyatithirbrāhmaṇo gṛhān । tasyaitāṃ śāntiṃ kurvanti hara vaivasvatodakam ॥ 7 ॥
verses.trans: A Brahmin guest enters like fire. To appease him, one must offer water. Bring water, O Death!

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Guest as the sacred seeker of knowledge.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Serving the seeker is serving the Divine.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Soul as an expansion of the Lord's light.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Flame of knowledge entering the house of ego.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: आशाप्रतीक्षे सङ्गतं सूनृतां चेष्टापूर्ते पुत्रपशूंश्च सर्वान् । एतद्वृङ्क्ते पुरुषस्याल्पमेधसो यस्याश्नन्वसति ब्राह्मणो गृहे ॥ ८॥
verses.iast: āśāpratīkṣe saṅgataṃ sūnṛtāṃ ceṣṭāpūrte putrapaśūṃśca sarvān । etadvṛṅkte puruṣasyālpamedhaso yasyāśnanvasati brāhmaṇo gṛhe ॥ 8 ॥
verses.trans: Neglecting a guest destroys the merits of the small-minded man.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Neglect of truth destroys ritual merit.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Merit found in reverence toward seekers.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Cost of failing to recognize divinity in others.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Ego ignoring higher aspiration burns its own growth.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: तिस्रो रात्रीर्यदवात्सीर्गृहे मेऽनश्नन्ब्रह्मन्नतिथिरनमस्यः । नमस्तेऽस्तु ब्रह्मन्स्वस्ति मेऽस्तु तस्मात्प्रति त्रीन्वरान्वृणीष्व ॥ ९॥
verses.iast: tisro rātrīryadavātsīrgṛhe me'naśnanbrahmannatithirnamasyaḥ । namaste'stu brahmansvasti me'stu tasmātprati trīnvarānvṛṇīṣva ॥ 9 ॥
verses.trans: Yama: 'Stayed three nights without food, choose three boons.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Nights represent periods of intense discipline.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Lord's response to sincerity.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Yama bound by cosmic law to compensate the seeker.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: शान्तसङ्कल्पः सुमना यथा स्याद्वीतमन्युर्गौतमो माभिमृत्यो । ... ॥ १०॥
verses.iast: śāntasaṅkalpaḥ sumanā yathā syādvītamanyurgautamo mābhi mṛtyo... ॥ 10 ॥
verses.trans: First Boon: May my father be calm and recognize me.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Seeking harmony with one's source.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Repairing worldly relationships before higher inquiry.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Purity of mind requested by the devotee.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Restoring inner balance for further ascent.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: यथा पुरस्ताद्भविता प्रतीत औद्दालकिरारुणिर्मत्प्रसृष्टः । ... ॥ ११॥
verses.iast: yathā purastād bhavitā pratīta auddālakirāruṇirmatprasṛṣṭaḥ... ॥ 11 ॥
verses.trans: Yama grants: Your father will know you as before, peaceful and loving.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Harmony restored by Divine favor.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Grace manifests as reconciliation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Divine order restored.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: स्वर्गे लोके न भयं किञ्चनास्ति न तत्र त्वं न जरया बिभेति । ... ॥ १२॥
verses.iast: svarge loke na bhayaṃ kiñcanāsti na tatra tvan na jarayā bibheti... ॥ 12 ॥
verses.trans: Second Boon: Teach me the fire sacrifice that leads to heaven, where there is no fear.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Heaven is still within samsara.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Celestial worlds under Divine control.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Disciplines to reach the Lord.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Ascent to higher planes of consciousness.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: स त्वमग्निं स्वर्ग्यमध्येषि मृत्यो प्रब्रूहि त्वं श्रद्दधानाय मह्यम् । ... ॥ १३॥
verses.iast: sa tvamagniṃ svargyamadhyeṣi mṛtyo prabrūhi tvaṃ śraddadhānāya mahyam... ॥ 13 ॥
verses.trans: You know that fire-sacrifice. Teach me, for I have faith.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Faith as a tool for knowledge.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Surrender to the teacher.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Commitment to the divine methodology.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: प्र ते ब्रवीमि तदु मे निबोध स्वर्ग्यमग्निं नचिकेतः प्रजानन् । ... ॥ १४॥
verses.iast: pra te bravīmi tadu me nibodha svargyamagniṃ naciketaḥ prajānan... ॥ 14 ॥
verses.trans: Yama: It is hidden in the secret cave of the heart.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Internal nature of the sacrifice.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Lord residing in the heart.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Sacred center of the devotee.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: लोकादिमग्निं तमुवाच तस्मै या इष्टका यावतीर्वा यथा वा । ... ॥ १५॥
verses.iast: lokādimagniṃ tamuvāca tasmai yā iṣṭakā yāvatīrvā yathā vā... ॥ 15 ॥
verses.trans: Yama taught him the bricks and the fire; Nachiketa repeated it perfectly.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Discipline brings mastery.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Perfect transmission of knowledge.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Appreciating the devotee's skill.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: तमब्रवीत्प्रीयमाणो महात्मा वरं तवेहाद्य ददामि भूयः । ... ॥ १६॥
verses.iast: tamabravītprīyamāṇo mahātmā varaṃ tavehādya dadāmi bhūyaḥ... ॥ 16 ॥
verses.trans: Yama: This fire shall be known by your name.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Student becomes one with the truth.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Divine recognition.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Lord's favor on the devotee.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Identification of the soul with the path.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: त्रिणाचिकेतस्त्रिभिरेत्य सन्धिं त्रिकर्मकृत्तरति जन्ममृत्यू । ... ॥ १७॥
verses.iast: triṇāchikētastribhirētya sandhiṃ trikarmakṛttarati janmamṛtyū... ॥ 17 ॥
verses.trans: Performing this thrice overcomes birth and death.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Transcendence of the cycle.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Grace conquers mortality.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Fulfillment of karmic duty.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: त्रिणाचिकेतस्त्रयमेतद्विद्वा य एवं विद्वाश्चिनुते नाचिकेतम् । ... ॥ १८॥
verses.iast: triṇāchikētastrayamētadviditvā ya ēvaṃ vidvāṃśchinutē nāchikētam... ॥ 18 ॥
verses.trans: He who knows this and performs the sacrifice casts off bonds.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Knowledge frees the seeker.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Understanding as path to liberation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Freedom through devotion.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: एष तेऽग्निनर्नाचिकेतः स्वर्ग्यो यमवृणीथा द्वितीयेन वरेण । ... ॥ १९॥
verses.iast: ēṣa tē'gnirnachikēta-ssvargyō yamavṛṇīthā dvitīyēna varēṇa... ॥ 19 ॥
verses.trans: Yama: This was your second boon. Choose your third.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Completion of preparatory steps.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Advancing toward the Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Moving to higher stages of grace.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: येयं प्रेते विचिकित्सा मनुष्येऽस्तीत्येके नायमस्तीति चैके । ... ॥ २०॥
verses.iast: yēya-mprētē vichikitsā manuṣyē-'stītyēkē nāyamastīti chaikē... ॥ 20 ॥
verses.trans: Third Boon: Does a person exist after death?

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The core existential dilemma.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Status of the soul in the beyond.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Nature of the soul's survival.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Question of consciousness after the form dissolves.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: देवैरत्रापि विचिकित्सितं पुरा न हि सुविज्ञेयमणुरेष धर्मः । ... ॥ २१॥
verses.iast: dēvairatrāpi vichikitsita-mpurā na hi suvijñēyamaṇurēṣa dharmaḥ... ॥ 21 ॥
verses.trans: Yama: Even gods doubt this. Choose another boon.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Testing the seeker's resolve.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The mystery is known only to the Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Difficulty of understanding the Divine nature.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: देवैरत्रापि विचिकित्सितं किल त्वञ्च मृत्यो यन्न सुज्ञेयमात्थ । ... ॥ २२॥
verses.iast: dēvairatrāpi vichikitsita-ṅkila tva-ñcha mṛtyō yanna sujñēyamāttha... ॥ 22 ॥
verses.trans: Nachiketa: None better than you can teach this.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Single-minded pursuit of truth.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Persistence of the devotee.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Trust in the teacher.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: शतायुषः पुत्रपौत्रान्वृणीष्व बहून्पशून्हस्तिहिरण्यमश्वान् । ... ॥ २३॥
verses.iast: śatāyuṣaḥ putrapautrānvṛṇīṣva bahūnpaśūnhastihiraṇyamaśvān... ॥ 23 ॥
verses.trans: Yama: Choose gold, horses, kingdoms, long life.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Temptations of the material mind.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Worldly allurements as obstacles.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Testing the soul's commitment.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: एतत्तुल्यं यदि मन्यसे वरं वृणीष्व वित्तं चिरजीविकाञ्च । ... ॥ २४॥
verses.iast: ētattulyaṃ yadi manyasē varaṃ vṛṇīṣva vitta-ñchirajīvikā-ñcha... ॥ 24 ॥
verses.trans: Yama: Take anything, but do not ask about the mystery of death.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Attempt to preserve duality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Worldly happiness is limited.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The Lord hides the Truth from the unprepared.
commentaries.integral_advaita: The world seeks to keep the soul satisfied in the manifest.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: ये ये कामा दुर्लभा मर्त्यलोके सर्वान्कामांश्छन्दतः प्रार्थयस्व । ... ॥ २५॥
verses.iast: yē yē kāmā durlabhā martyalōkē sarvānkāmāṃśChandataḥ prārthayasva... ॥ 25 ॥
verses.trans: Yama: Take these beautiful women and chariots.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Pleasure as a distraction.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Material satisfaction as a snare.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Transient worldly gains.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: श्वोभावा मर्त्यस्य यदन्तकैतत्सर्वेन्द्रियाणां जरयन्ति तेजः । ... ॥ २६॥
verses.iast: śvōbhāvā martyasya yadantakaitatsarvēndriyāṇā-ñjarayanti tējaḥ... ॥ 26 ॥
verses.trans: Nachiketa: These wear out the vigor of the senses.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Dispassion through reasoning.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Knowledge of the impermanent.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Wisdom of the devotee.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Realization of the futility of externalism.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: न वित्तेन तर्पणीयो मनुष्यो लप्स्याामहे वित्तमद्राक्ष्म चेत्त्वा । ... ॥ २७॥
verses.iast: na vittēna tarpaṇīyō manuṣyō lapsyāmahē vittamadrākśma chēttvā... ॥ 27 ॥
verses.trans: Nachiketa: Wealth cannot satisfy man.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Atman is the only source of joy.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Only the Lord satisfies.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Devotion as the only true wealth.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: अजीर्यताममृतानामुपेत्य जीर्यन्मर्त्यः क्वधःस्थः प्रजानन् । ... ॥ २८॥
verses.iast: ajīryatāmamṛtānāmupētya jīryanmartyaḥ kvadhassthaḥ prajānan... ॥ 28 ॥
verses.trans: Nachiketa: Who would take delight in a long life of song?

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Renunciation is natural to the wise.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Seeking the eternal is wise.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Spiritual maturity.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 1 — Nachiketa Meets Yama
verses.dev: यस्मिन्निदं विचिकित्सन्ति मृत्यो यत्साम्परायें महति ब्रूहि नस्तत् । ... ॥ २९॥
verses.iast: yasminnidaṃ vichikitsanti mṛtyō yatsāmparāyē mahati brūhi nastat... ॥ 29 ॥
verses.trans: Nachiketa: I choose no other boon than the mystery of death.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Perfect qualifications for realization.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Total surrender to the Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Purest devotion.
commentaries.integral_advaita: The soul's absolute claim on Truth.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: अन्यच्छ्रेयोऽन्यदुतैव प्रेयस्ते उभे नानार्थे पुरुषं सिनीतः । तयोः श्रेय आददानस्य साधु भवति हीयतेऽर्थाद्य उ प्रेयो वृणीते ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: anyacchreyo'nyadutaiva preyaste ubhe nānārthe puruṣaṃ sinītaḥ । tayoḥ śreya ādadānasya sādhu bhavati hīyate'rthādya u preyo vrṛṇīte ॥ 1 ॥
verses.trans: Yama said: One thing is the good (Shreyas); quite another is the pleasant (Preyas). Both of these, serving different purposes, bind a man. It goes well with him who clings to the good; but he who chooses the pleasant misses the true end of life.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that Shreyas (liberation/Vidya) and Preyas (worldly enjoyment/Avidya) stand in absolute opposition. They approach human beings as alternative choices. Shreyas leads to internal freedom, while Preyas traps the intellect in sensory loops. A human being cannot pursue both simultaneously; choosing one requires turning away from the other.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja explains that Shreyas represents actions that align the soul with God's eternal service, while Preyas represents pursuits driven by sensory ego and short-term bodily identification. Choosing Preyas alienates the Jiva from its divine destiny.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: Swami Vivekananda popularized this verse to show that the ethical foundation of humanity depends on our ability to choose what is permanently beneficial over what is temporarily pleasant. This is the definition of true strength and character.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: श्रेयश्च प्रेयश्च मनुष्यमेतस्तौ सम्परीत्य विविनक्ति धीरः । श्रेयो हि धीरोऽभि प्रेयसो वृणीते प्रेयो मन्दो योगक्षेमाद्वृणीते ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: śreyaśca preyaśca manuṣyametastau samparītya vivinakti dhīraḥ । śreyo hi dhīro'bhi preyaso vrṛṇīte preyo mando yogakṣemādvṛṇīte ॥ 2 ॥
verses.trans: Both the good and the pleasant approach man. The wise man examines them closely and discriminates between the two. The wise man prefers the good to the pleasant, whereas the foolish man chooses the pleasant for the sake of body-welfare and greed.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara draws a vivid analogy of a swan separating milk from water. The 'Dhira' (the deeply steady, discriminative individual) uses intense intellectual analysis to separate the transient from the permanent. The dull-witted person ('Manda') lacks this discernment and chooses comfort, falling prey to 'Yoga-kshema' (acquiring and protecting material possessions).
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The discriminating faculty is a gift from the Lord to be activated by pure living. The foolish man is consumed by bodily maintenance and preservation, forgetting that the physical body is merely an instrument, not the ultimate goal.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights that the choice of 'Manda' is driven entirely by immediate sensory gratification. True intelligence lies in recognizing that real security is not material but spiritual, stemming from the grace of the Supreme Lord.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: स त्वं प्रियान्प्रियरूपांश्च कामानभिध्यायन्नचिकेतोऽत्यस्राक्षीः । नैतां सृङ्कां वित्तमयीमवाप्तो यस्यां मज्जन्ति बहवः मनुष्याः ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: sa tvaṃ priyānpriyarūpāṃśca kāmānabhidhyāyannaciketo'tyasrākṣīḥ । naitāṃ sṛṅkāṃ vittamayīmavāpto yasyāṃ majjanti bahavaḥ manuṣyāḥ ॥ 3 ॥
verses.trans: O Nachiketa, having deeply reflected upon all those pleasures that are desirable and lovely to look at, thou hast discarded them all. Thou hast refused to accept that golden chain of wealth in which so many multitudes of men sink and drown.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Yama praises Nachiketa's extraordinary maturity. Shankara points out that even when presented with supernatural, celestial enjoyments, Nachiketa recognized them as structural bondages ('Srnka'). By turning them down, Nachiketa broke through the initial layer of cosmic illusion.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The 'chain of wealth' is that which causes standard human beings to lose their spiritual sanity. Nachiketa's ability to reject it indicates a heart fully purified and ready for the highest instructions on divine love and absolute truth.
commentaries.universalist: Eknath Easwaran remarks that Nachiketa bypassed the consumerist trap that catches most of humanity. He looked at the best the world had to offer and calmly stated: 'This is not enough because it does not last.'

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: दूरमेते विपरीते विषूची अविद्या या च विद्येति ज्ञाता । विद्याभीप्सिनं नचिकेतसं मन्ये न त्वा कामा बहवोऽलोलुपन्त ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: dūramete viparīte viṣūcī avidyā yā ca vidyeti jñātā । vidyābhīpsinaṃ naciketasaṃ manye na tvā kāmā bahavo'lolupanta ॥ 4 ॥
verses.trans: Wide apart and leading to entirely different directions are these two: Ignorance (Avidya) and Knowledge (Vidya). I regard thee, Nachiketa, as an authentic seeker of knowledge, because even a vast multitude of desires could not distract thee.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Avidya (Ignorance) binds one to the objective universe and cycles of rebirth, whereas Vidya (Knowledge) reveals the absolute subjective reality. Shankara emphasizes that Yama officially certifies Nachiketa as an authorized container ('Adhikari') for Brahmavidya.
commentaries.integral_yoga: Sri Aurobindo notes that Vidya and Avidya are two cosmic powers. Avidya is the consciousness of the multi-fissured, divided material plane; Vidya is the unifying vision of the One. Nachiketa belongs completely to the kingdom of the One.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Knowledge is that which reveals the supreme majesty of God and the soul's relationship to Him. Material temptations are the tests applied by cosmic administrators; passing them clears the path for authentic spiritual initiation.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: अविद्यायामन्तरे वर्तमानाः स्वयं धीराः पण्डितं मन्यमानाः । दन्द्रम्यमाणाः परियन्ति मूढा अन्धेनैव नीयमाना यथान्धाः ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: avidyāyāmantare vartamānāḥ svyaṃ dhīrāḥ paṇḍitaṃ manyamānāḥ । dandramyamāṇāḥ pariyanti mūḍhā andhenaiva nīyamānā yathāndhāḥ ॥ 5 ॥
verses.trans: Dwelling in the very midst of ignorance, yet considering themselves wise and highly learned, fools go round and round in staggered circles, like blind men led by the blind.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: This is a scathing critique of intellectual arrogance without spiritual depth. Shankara explains that people who excel entirely in material science or dry ritualism believe they are 'Pandits' (enlightened scholars). They twist and turn in psychological misery because their foundational premise of reality is incorrect.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: Swami Sarvapriyananda highlights that dry secular knowledge without subjective awareness simply compounds our existential confusion. Following a secular guide who is himself spiritually unawakened ensures a continuation of systemic delusion.
commentaries.universalist: The phrase 'blind leading the blind' originated structurally in the Upanishads long before entering Western vernacular, capturing the timeless pattern of human societies imitating each other's superficial behaviors.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: न साम्परायः प्रतिभाति बालं प्रमाद्यन्तं वित्तमोहेन मूढम् । अयं लोको नास्ति पर इति मानी पुनः पुनः वशमापद्यते मे ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: na sāmparāyaḥ pratibhāti bālaṃ pramādyantaṃ vittamohena mūḍham । ayaṃ loko nāsti para iti mānī punaḥ punaḥ vaśamāpद्यte me ॥ 6 ॥
verses.trans: The path to the hereafter is never apparent to the childish, careless mind, deluded by the glamour of wealth. Thinking 'This world alone exists, and there is no other,' such a person falls again and again under my sway.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: 'Samparayah' means the spiritual path leading to liberation, or the higher worlds. The child-like or immature mind ('Balam') is blinded by the immediate feedback loop of money and sensory comfort. Such materialists conclude that what cannot be seen or touched does not exist, ensuring they remain bound to the cycle of physical mortality governed by Yama.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Denying the higher transcendental realities and living purely for bodily pleasures cuts off the Jiva's access to the divine realm. The soul remains chained to mundane physical cycles, suffering the painful consequences of repeated births and deaths.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva observes that 'under my sway' means that Yama is forced to punish and recycle these deluded souls according to their karmic debts. Wealth creates a psychological thicket that blocks the clear light of devotion to God.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: श्रवणायापि बहुभिर्यो न लभ्यः शृण्वन्तोऽपि बहवो यं न विदुः । आश्चर्यो वक्ता कुशलोऽस्य लब्धाश्चर्यो ज्ञाता कुशलानुशिष्टः ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: śravaṇāyāpi bahubhiryo na labhyaḥ śṛṇvanto'pi bahvo yaṃ na viduḥ । āścaryo vaktā kuśalo'sya labdhāścaryo jñātā kuśalānuśiṣṭaḥ ॥ 7 ॥
verses.trans: He (the Self) is difficult to even hear about for many; and even among those who hear, many do not comprehend. Wondrous is the speaker who can explain It; rare and talented is the seeker who attains It; wonderful indeed is the knower who is instructed by an illumined teacher.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara points out that Self-realization is a triple miracle. It requires a rare, fully realized Guru to articulate it cleanly, an exceptionally focused student to grasp its subtle subjective direction, and a pure mind to retain the truth of non-duality amidst a world of apparent diversity.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The soul's nature is so deep and distinct from matter that standard human language fails to adequately describe it. It can only be communicated effectively through a lineage of pure teachers who have experienced the divine presence firsthand.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: Vivekananda used this verse to show that religion is a matter of direct realization, not a matter of intellectual assent or memorizing books. It is an extraordinary psychological breakthrough.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: न नरेणावरेण प्रोक्त एष सुविज्ञेयो बहुधा चिन्त्यमानः । अनन्यप्रोक्ते गतिरत्र नास्त्यणीयान् ह्यतर्क्यमणुप्रमाणात् ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: na nareṇāvareṇa prokta eṣa suvijñeyo bahudhā cintyamānaḥ । ananyaprokte gatirahtra nāstyaṇīyān hyatarkyamaṇupramāṇāt ॥ 8 ॥
verses.trans: Taught by an inferior teacher, this Self cannot be truly comprehended, for It is thought of in many different ways. But when taught by a teacher who is identical with It, there is no further doubt. It is subtler than the subtlest and beyond the reach of mere argumentative logic.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara clarifies 'Ananya-prokte' (taught by one who is non-different). When a Guru has realized their absolute identity with the Atman, their words carry an transmission of truth that instantly dismantles the illusion of duality. An unawakened teacher merely creates intellectual concepts, leading to endless arguments.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: An 'inferior teacher' is one driven by personal pride or material motives. The true nature of the soul can only be illuminated by someone who speaks from a position of absolute submission to the Supreme Divine Reality.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Argumentation alone cannot map God or the soul. 'Ananya' can also mean pointing exclusively to the Supreme Lord as the unique, independent ruler of all creation. This insight terminates all philosophical wandering.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: नैषा तर्केण मतिरापनेया प्रोक्तान्येनैव सुज्ञानाय प्रेष्ठ । यां त्वमापः सत्यधृतिर्बतासि त्वादृङ्नो भूयान्नचिकेतः प्रष्टा ॥ ९ ॥
verses.iast: naiṣā tarkeṇa matirāpaneyā proktānyenaiva sujñānāya preṣṭha । yāṃ tvamāpaḥ satyadhṛtirbatāsi tvādṛṅno bhūyānnaciketaḥ praṣṭā ॥ 9 ॥
verses.trans: This spiritual conviction cannot be attained through intellect and logical debate, O dearest Nachiketa; It becomes easy to understand only when taught by another. Thou hast attained this conviction through absolute steadfastness in truth. May we always find a questioner like thee!

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara directly emphasizes that standard logic ('Tarka') is structurally flawed for realizing the Self because logic requires a subject-object duality (an observer analyzing an observed thing). Since the Atman is the ultimate witness, it can never be made an object of logic. It requires intuitive recognition sparked by a realized guide.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Conviction ('Mati') arises when the mind is pacified through devotion and moral discipline. Yama looks at Nachiketa with genuine admiration, celebrating his 'Satya-dhriti' (unwavering commitment to truth), which is the absolute baseline for advanced spiritual growth.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: This verse underscores that spiritual inquiry is completely different from dry philosophical speculation. It demands real personal character, emotional integrity, and an authentic thirst for reality.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: जानाम्यहं शेवधिरित्यनित्यं न ह्यध्रुवैः प्राप्यते ही ध्रुवं तत् । ततो मया नाचिकेतश्चितोऽग्निरनित्यैर्द्रव्यैः प्राप्तवानस्मि नित्यम् ॥ १० ॥
verses.iast: jānāmyahaṃ śevadhirityanityāṃ na hyadhruvaiḥ prāpyate hī dhruvaṃ tat । tato mayā nāciketaścito'gniranityairdravyaiḥ prāptavānasmi nityam ॥ 10 ॥
verses.trans: I know that earthly treasure is transient, and that the Eternal can never be attained by that which is temporary. Yet, I have performed the Nachiketa Fire-sacrifice using transient objects, and through it, I have attained this enduring position of cosmic office.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara handles a subtle point here: Yama is explaining that his own position as the Lord of Death is a highly elevated celestial office attained via ritual action (Karma). However, even this grand cosmic status is relatively transient ('Anityam') compared to the absolute, unconditioned freedom of pure Self-realization. Yama uses his own position to illustrate the absolute limits of ritual action.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Worldly wealth and ritual actions are 'adhruva' (unstable). They can buy temporary positions in the cosmic hierarchy, but they cannot buy absolute liberation. Yama acknowledges that his position is temporary, showing his deep spiritual humility.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that Yama has attained his position through dedicated cosmic service to Vishnu. He uses his own case to show that while righteous karma can earn great cosmic rewards, the absolute mystery of the soul requires going deeper than external actions.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: कामस्याप्तिं जगतः प्रतिष्ठां क्रतोरानन्त्यमभयस्य पारम् । स्तोममहदुरुगायं प्रतिष्ठां दृष्ट्वा धृत्या धीरो नचिकेतोऽत्यस्राक्षीः ॥ ११ ॥
verses.iast: kāmasyāptiṃ jagataḥ pratiṣṭhāṃ kratorānantyamabhayasya pāram । stomamahadurugāyaṃ pratiṣṭhāṃ dṛṣṭvā dhṛtyā dhīro naciketo'tyasrākṣīḥ ॥ 11 ॥
verses.trans: The fulfillment of all desires, the very foundation of the universe, the endless rewards of rituals, the shore where fear ends, that grand and wide refuge which is highly praised—thou hast looked upon all these with profound wisdom, O Nachiketa, and deliberately discarded them with ultimate resolve.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Yama outlines the highest state of material and celestial manifestation—Hiranyagarbha (the cosmic mind). Even though Nachiketa had the opportunity to become a cosmic master of this entire structure, his discriminative insight allowed him to see that it was still within the realm of time and modification. He pushed past the universe to find the source.
commentaries.integral_yoga: The 'shore where fear ends' within the material universe is only a psychological reflection. True absolute fearlessness belongs exclusively to the unconditioned divine soul. Nachiketa's rejection of cosmic supremacy proves his perfect alignment with the absolute divine essence.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: This marks the highest praise a teacher can give a student. Nachiketa has looked at the absolute zenith of worldly success and ambition, recognized its structural limitations, and calmly walked away from it.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: तं दुर्दर्शं गूढमनुप्रविष्टं गुहाहितं गहरेष्ठं पुराणम् । अध्यात्मयोगाधिगमेन देवं मत्वा धीरो हर्षशोकौ जहाति ॥ १२ ॥
verses.iast: taṃ durdarśaṃ gūḍhamanupraviṣṭaṃ guhāhitaṃ gahareṣṭhaṃ purāṇam । adhyātmayogādhigamena devaṃ matvā dhīro harṣaśokau jahāti ॥ 12 ॥
verses.trans: Realizing through the practice of inner meditation (Adhyatma Yoga) that ancient, effulgent God who is difficult to perceive, deeply hidden, dwelling in the secret cavity of the heart, and seated in the midst of dark mysteries—the wise man completely leaves behind both joy and sorrow.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara provides the foundational methodology of Vedanta. The Atman is 'Gudham' (hidden) because it cannot be seen by the senses. It is 'Guhahitvam' because it resides within the intellect as the ultimate subject. By practicing 'Adhyatma-yoga'—withdrawing the mind from external objects and plunging into pure awareness—one transcends the emotional dualities of pleasure ('Harsha') and pain ('Shoka').
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The 'Devam' refers to the Antaryamin—the divine Lord dwelling as the inner soul of the individual Jiva. Through dedicated meditation, the seeker perceives this divine presence within, causing all earthly dualities to melt into an ocean of peace.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that the soul learns to recognize its complete dependence on the Supreme Being who resides deep within the heart. This recognition frees the Jiva from the emotional volatility of material existence.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: एतच्छ्रुत्वा सम्परिगृह्य मर्त्यः प्रवृह्य धर्म्यमणुमेतमाप्य । स मोदते मोदनीयं हि लब्ध्वा विवृतं सद्म नचिकेतसं मन्ये ॥ १३ ॥
verses.iast: etacchrutvā samparigṛhya martyaḥ pravṛhya dharmyamaṇumetamāpya । sa modate modanīyaṃ hi labdhvā vivṛtaṃ sadma naciketasaṃ manye ॥ 13 ॥
verses.trans: The mortal who has heard this truth, grasped it firmly, separated this subtle essence of righteousness from all external forms, and realized It, rejoices because he has obtained the source of all true delight. I believe that the gates of the eternal home stand wide open for thee, O Nachiketa!

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: 'Pravrihya' means separating or extracting the Self from the physical body-mind complex, just as one extracts a blade of grass from its sheath. Upon realizing this subtle, unconditioned reality, the Jiva experiences the true, native bliss of consciousness. Yama declares that Nachiketa’s mind is perfectly prepared for this entry.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The source of all delight is the Lord Himself. Grasping this truth means separating the eternal reality from the changing material shell. Yama recognizes Nachiketa as a soul whose path back to the divine kingdom is completely clear.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: Spiritual life is defined here as a process of conscious extraction—deliberately pulling your sense of identity out of your passing thoughts and physical form, and anchoring it firmly in pure consciousness.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: अन्यत्र धर्मादन्यत्राधर्मादन्यत्रास्मात्कृताकृतात् । अन्यत्र भूताच्च भव्याच्च यत्तत्पश्यसि तद्वद ॥ १४ ॥
verses.iast: anyatra dharmādanyatrādharmādanyatrāsmātkṛtākṛtāt । anyatra bhūtācca bhavyācca yattatpaśyasi tadvada ॥ 14 ॥
verses.trans: (Nachiketa said) Tell me that which thou seest as beyond virtue (Dharma) and vice (Adharma), beyond cause (Krita) and effect (Akrita), and beyond past and future.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: This is one of the most celebrated questions in philosophical history. Nachiketa is asking to know the Absolute Reality that is completely unconditioned by moral dualities (Dharma/Adharma), causal loops (Krita/Akrita), and the structural matrix of time (past/future). This is a direct inquiry into the nature of Nirguna Brahman—the unconditioned Absolute.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Nachiketa seeks to understand that supreme divine state which transcends the standard rules of material karma, cause and effect, and the mechanical passage of time. He wants to know the essential nature of the highest reality.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: Swami Sarvapriyananda notes that this question maps the absolute boundary of human philosophy. True reality must exist outside of time, outside of causality, and outside of relative moral dualities. Nachiketa is demanding the ultimate core truth.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: सर्वे वेदा यत्पदमामनन्ति तपांसि सर्वाणि च यद्वदन्ति । यदिच्छन्तो ब्रह्मचर्यं चरन्ति तत्ते पदं सङ्ग्रहेण ब्रवीम्योमित्येतत् ॥ १५ ॥
verses.iast: sarve vedā yatpadamāmananti tapāṃsi sarvāṇi ca yadvandanti । yadicchanto brahmacaryaṃ caranti tatte padaṃ saṅgraheṇa bravīmyomityetat ॥ 15 ॥
verses.trans: (Yama said) That goal which all the Vedas declare, which all austerities point toward, and desiring which men lead a life of spiritual discipline (Brahmacharya)—that goal I tell thee briefly: It is OM.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Yama begins his answer by providing the ultimate sound-symbol of reality: OM (Pranava). Shankara explains that OM is both the symbol (Pratika) and the direct gateway to both the relative Brahman and the supreme Absolute. It is the sonic distillation of all scriptural wisdom and human striving.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: OM is the primordial sound representing the Supreme Lord Narayana. All Vedic rituals and lifestyle disciplines like Brahmacharya are intended to align the individual consciousness with the divine resonance of this ultimate syllable.
commentaries.universalist: OM represents the foundational vibration of the entire universe. By condensing all spiritual practices into this single syllable, the Upanishad provides a universal, experiential focus point for all human concentration.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: एतद्ध्येवाक्षरं ब्रह्म एतद्ध्येवाक्षरं परम् । एतद्ध्येवाक्षरं ज्ञात्वा यो यदिच्छति तस्य तत् ॥ १६ ॥
verses.iast: etaddhyevākṣaraṃ brahma etaddhyevākṣaraṃ param । etaddhyevākṣaraṃ jñātvā yo yadicchati tasya tat ॥ 16 ॥
verses.trans: This syllable is indeed Brahman; this syllable is the highest Absolute. Knowing this sacred syllable, whatever one desires becomes theirs.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara clarifies that 'Avara Brahman' (the manifested universe) and 'Para Brahman' (the unmanifested Absolute) are both accessible through OM. If a seeker meditates on OM as the relative world, they gain cosmic mastery; if they realize OM as pure, unconditioned awareness, they gain absolute liberation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The syllable OM contains the keys to both material blessings and absolute salvation. It depends entirely upon the internal purity and intention of the practitioner when they tune into this divine sound.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: OM is the unique name of the Supreme Lord Vishnu. Knowing this name with full comprehension of its theological depth allows a soul to achieve its spiritual goals through the direct grace of God.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: एतदालम्बनं श्रेष्ठमेतदालम्बनं परम् । एतदालम्बनं ज्ञात्वा ब्रह्मलोके महीयते ॥ १७ ॥
verses.iast: etadālambanaṃ śreṣṭhametadālambanaṃ param । etadālambanaṃ jñātvā brahmaloke mahīyate ॥ 17 ॥
verses.trans: This is the safest support; this is the highest refuge. Knowing this support, one becomes glorified in the realm of Brahman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: 'Alambanam' means a mental anchor or support. Because the human mind cannot easily conceive of unconditioned, formless awareness, OM serves as the absolute physical and auditory bridge. Anchoring the mind to it leads to the direct realization of absolute unity.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja emphasizes that meditating on OM with devotion allows the soul to transcend lower material states and attain entry into the spiritual kingdom of the Lord, where it experiences eternal bliss.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: The mind requires a concrete focus point to cultivate silence. This verse presents the sacred vibration as the premier psychological tool to steady the mind, leading it from noise to deep meditation.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: न जायते म्रियते वा विपश्चिन्नायं कुतश्चिन्न बभूव कश्चित् । अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणो न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे ॥ १८ ॥
verses.iast: na jāyate mriyate vā vipaścinnāyaṃ kutaścinna babhūva kaścit । ajo nityaḥ śāśvato'yaṃ purāṇo na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre ॥ 18 ॥
verses.trans: The knowing Self (Atman) is never born, nor does It ever die. It did not spring from anything, and nothing ever sprang from It. It is unborn, eternal, everlasting, and ancient. It is never destroyed when the physical body is destroyed.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: This is one of the most famous verses in all of Hindu scripture, duplicated almost verbatim by Sri Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita (2.20). Shankara explains that the word 'Vipashcit' means pure, inherent consciousness. Birth and death are merely modifications of physical matter. The subjective awareness within has no cause and no effect; It simply IS.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The individual soul (Jiva) is eternal and uncreated, existing as an organic part of God's body. While the physical shell goes through biological evolution, decay, and death, the conscious spirit remains entirely unaffected.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The soul is distinctly separate from the material frame. Its eternal nature is guaranteed by the continuous protective will of the Supreme Lord, ensuring that no physical weapon or material destruction can dissolve the individual spirit.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: हन्ता चेन्मन्यते हन्तुं हतश्चेन्मन्यते हतम् । उभौ तौ न विजानीतो नायं हन्ति न हन्यते ॥ १९ ॥
verses.iast: hantā cenmanyate hantuṃ hataścenmanyate hatam । ubhau tau na vijānīto nāyaṃ hanti na hanyate ॥ 19 ॥
verses.trans: If the killer thinks he kills, or if the slain thinks he is killed, both of them are deeply mistaken. This Self neither kills, nor can It ever be killed.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The illusion of killing and dying arises entirely from identifying oneself with the physical body. Shankara emphasizes that the absolute Self is completely actionless ('Akarta'). Actions belong exclusively to the realm of nature (Prakriti). The True Self is merely the immutable witness of the drama.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The physical body is subject to destruction, but the soul cannot be structurally altered. The verse serves as a moral and metaphysical correction against equating human identity entirely with the flesh.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: This insight provides the baseline for absolute fearlessness. If you recognize that your core identity is pure awareness rather than your physical frame, the primary fear of physical destruction completely evaporates from your psyche.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: अणोरणीयान्महतो महीयानात्मास्य जन्तोर्निहितो गुहायाम् । तमक्रतुः पश्यति वीतशोको धातुप्रसादान्महिमानमात्मनः ॥ २० ॥
verses.iast: aṇoraṇīyānmahato mahīyānātmāsya jantornihito guhāyām । tamakratuḥ paśyati vītaśoko dhātuprasādānamahimānamātmanaḥ ॥ 20 ॥
verses.trans: Subtler than the subtlest, greater than the greatest, the Self is hidden in the secret heart of all living creatures. A person who is completely free from desire and personal ambition beholds the majesty of the Self through the purification of the mind and senses, and thus transcends all sorrow.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The Atman transcends all concepts of spatial dimensions. It is smaller than an atom because it is the subtle subject behind the atom; it is larger than the cosmos because the entire cosmos exists within consciousness. 'Akrathu' means one whose worldly desires have been completely quieted, allowing the mind to naturally reveal the light of the Self.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja interprets 'Dhatu-prasadat' explicitly as the *grace of the Supreme Creator*. The glory of the inner self cannot be conquered by sheer willpower alone; it is revealed when the individual soul surrenders to the Lord, who purifies the devotee's senses.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The Supreme Lord resides alongside the Jiva inside the heart. By 'Dhatu-prasada' (the grace of the independent Ruler), the dependent soul is granted a vision of God's immense majesty, terminating all existential grief.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: आसीनो दूरं व्रजति शयानो याति सर्वतः । कस्तं मदामदं देवं मदन्यो ज्ञातुमर्हति ॥ २१ ॥
verses.iast: āsīno dūraṃ vrajati śayāno yāti sarvataḥ । kastaṃ madāmadaṃ devaṃ madanyo jñātumarhati ॥ 21 ॥
verses.trans: Sitting still, It travels far; lying down, It goes everywhere. Who else but myself can know that effulgent God who is filled with both joy and joylessness (transcendence)?

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains these logical paradoxes beautifully. The Atman is omnipresent. When the physical body sits still, the mind can think of distant galaxies—this occurs because consciousness is already there. 'Madamadam' means it contains all relative dualities (joy/sorrow) while remaining completely untouched by them. Yama states that only a mind as perfectly balanced as his can map this paradox.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The paradoxes show that the Divine Ruler cannot be measured by physical laws of locomotion. He is simultaneously stationary in His supreme abode and intimately present within every atom of creation.
commentaries.integral_yoga: Consciousness is not bound by spatial coordinates. It can operate globally while remaining anchored locally. This verse describes the absolute freedom of unconditioned awareness.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: अशरीरं शरीरेष्वनवस्थेष्ववस्थितम् । महान्तं विभुमात्मानं मत्वा धीरो न शोचति ॥ २२ ॥
verses.iast: aśarīraṃ śarīreṣvanavastheṣvavasthitam । mahāntaṃ vibhumātmānaṃ matvā dhīro na śocati ॥ 22 ॥
verses.trans: Recognizing the Self as bodiless in the midst of bodies, as unchanging and permanent in the midst of changing things, the wise man transcends all sorrow.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The ultimate truth is that consciousness is 'Achariram' (inherently formless). It temporarily appears to inhabit a changing physical shell ('Anavastheshu'). Once the 'Dhira' understands that they are the immutable screen upon which the movie of the body is projected, grief becomes an impossibility.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The soul is an eternal conscious spark that moves through various transient biological physical forms based on its karmic evolution. Realizing its true nature as an immortal servant of the Divine eliminates the trauma of physical transition.
commentaries.universalist: This verse presents a profound psychological shift: anchoring your identity in the container (awareness) rather than the contents (the changing body, thoughts, and environment).

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: नायमात्मा प्रवचनेन लभ्यो न मेधया न बहुना श्रुतेन । यमेवैष वृणुते तेन लभ्यस्तस्यैष आत्मा विवृणुते तनूं स्वाम् ॥ २३ ॥
verses.iast: nāyamātmā pravacanena labhyo na medhayā na bahunā śrutena । yamevaiṣa vrṛṇute tena labhyastasyeṣa ātmā vivrṛṇute tanūṃ svām ॥ 23 ॥
verses.trans: This Self cannot be attained through continuous scriptural discourse, nor through intellectual power, nor through vast academic learning. It is attained only by him whom the Self chooses. To such a person, the Self reveals Its true nature.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara provides a non-dual interpretation of 'choosing'. The 'Self' and the 'seeker' are not two different entities. The seeker *is* the Self. Therefore, the phrase means that when a person turns away from external objects and chooses the Atman exclusively, that intense desire is the Self choosing itself. It is a process of total subjective focus.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: This is a cornerstone verse for Ramanuja's path of devotion (Bhakti). He explains that the Atman cannot be conquered by raw intellectual muscle or academic scholarship. The Lord selects and reveals Himself to those who love Him with absolute, unconditional surrender. Divine election and grace are paramount.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva asserts that liberation is entirely a gift of divine grace ('Prasada'). The Supreme Lord Vishnu chooses to reveal His form only to those souls who have demonstrated complete, unalloyed dedication and love.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: नाविरतो दुश्चरितान्नाशान्तो नासमाहितः । नाशान्तमानसो वापि प्रज्ञानेनैनमाप्नुयात् ॥ २४ ॥
verses.iast: nāvirato duścaritānnāśānto nāsamāhitaḥ । nāśāntamānaso vāpi prajñānenainamāpnuyāt ॥ 24 ॥
verses.trans: He who has not turned away from wicked conduct, who is not peaceful, who is distracted, and whose mind is not quieted, can never attain this Self through mere intellectual knowledge.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara establishes that ethical purity is a non-negotiable prerequisite for non-dual realization. Academic smarts ('Prajnana' here meaning mere intellectual information) cannot substitute for character. If a person continues to act wickedly or lacks emotional control, their mind remains too turbulent to reflect the silent light of the Atman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Spiritual progress requires complete ethical alignment. A mind troubled by malicious desires or sensory agitation cannot experience the presence of the Antaryamin.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: This verse acts as a warning against 'sofa-cushion philosophy'—intellectualizing spiritual oneness while maintaining a chaotic, undisciplined lifestyle. Transformation must be total, emotional, and behavioral.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 2 — Śreyas and Preyas
verses.dev: यस्य ब्रह्म च क्षत्रं च उभे भवत ओदनः । मृत्युर्यस्योपसेचनं क इत्था वेद यत्र सः ॥ २५ ॥
verses.iast: yasya brahma ca kṣatraṃ ca ubhe bhavata odanaḥ । mṛtyuryasyopasecanaṃ ka itthā veda yatra saḥ ॥ 25 ॥
verses.trans: To whom the priesthood (Brahma) and the warrior caste (Kshatra) are but ordinary food, and to whom Death itself is merely a condiment—who can truly know where that Supreme Self stands?

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The second section ends with a staggering cosmic image. Shankara explains that the priesthood and the ruling elites represent the absolute pillars of social order and material preservation. The Supreme Brahman consumes the entire manifested universe during cosmic dissolution (Pralaya), and treats Death itself as a minor seasoning ('Upasecanam' / pickle). It highlights the absolute transcendence of the Absolute.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The verse illustrates the awesome, terrifying scale of the Supreme Lord as the cosmic destroyer of all material designations. No human status or material protection can withstand the absolute authority of the Divine Creator.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets this as a declaration of the absolute sovereignty of Vishnu. Even the highest administrative forces of human society and the terrifying power of death are completely subordinate, functioning as mere instruments within His cosmic cycle.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: ऋतं पिबन्तौ सुकृतस्य लोके गुहां प्रविष्टौ परमे परार्धे । छायातपौ ब्रह्मविदो वदन्ति पञ्चाग्नयो ये च त्रिणाचिकेताः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: ṛtaṃ pibantau sukṛtasya loke guhāṃ praviṣṭau parame parārdhe । chāyātapau brahmavido vadanti pañcāgnayo ye ca triṇāciketāḥ ॥ 1 ॥
verses.trans: There are two souls that drink the fruits of their own true deeds in the world, having entered into the secret cave of the heart, which is the highest seat of the Supreme. The knowers of Brahman call them shade and light, as do those householders who maintain the five sacrificial fires and have thrice performed the Nachiketa fire-sacrifice.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara addresses a classic logical challenge: The Absolute Self (Paramatman) does not harvest karmic results, yet the verse says 'two drink.' He explains this through the law of association (like two riders on a horse where only one holds the reins). The two are the individual ego-soul (Jivatman), which experiences karma, and the Supreme Self (Paramatman), which acts as the silent, illuminating witness. They coexist in the cavity of the intellect like shade and light.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja emphasizes that the Jiva and the Antaryamin (the indwelling Lord) are distinct but structurally inseparable. Both dwell inside the heart. The Jiva experiences the direct consequences of its choices, while the Supreme Lord accompanies it out of boundless compassion, guiding it toward spiritual maturity.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: This verse establishes that our inner psychology is split between two identities: the surface ego that suffers and enjoys, and the deep, silent watcher that remains unconditioned. Spiritual life is the shift from the actor to the watcher.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: यः सेतुरीजानानामक्षरं ब्रह्म यत्परम् । अभयं तितीर्षतां पारं नाचिकेतँ शकेमहि ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: yaḥ seturījānānāmakṣaraṃ brahma yatparam । abhayaṃ titīrṣatāṃ pāraṃ nāciketa~ś akemahi ॥ 2 ॥
verses.trans: May we be master of that Nachiketa fire-sacrifice which is a bridge for those who perform rituals, and may we also know the imperishable, supreme Brahman, which is the ultimate, fearless shore for those who wish to cross the ocean of cyclic existence (Samsara).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Yama acts here as a compassionate guide, showing that both paths are valid depending on the seeker's evolution. The Nachiketa sacrifice is the bridge across material poverty to celestial wealth, while the realization of the absolute, imperishable Brahman is the definitive crossing out of time and space altogether.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The path consists of intermediate steps. Performing duty without personal attachment serves as a bridge that purifies the psyche, eventually making the soul fit to behold the supreme, fearless nature of God.
commentaries.universalist: This verse highlights the continuity of spiritual efforts. Whether one uses relative practices (like rituals and structured meditation) or direct inquiry, the destination is the transformation of consciousness from fear to absolute fearlessness.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: आत्मानँ रथिनं विद्धि शरीरँ रथमेव तु । बुद्धिं तु सारथिं विद्धि मनः प्रग्रहमेव च ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: ātmāna~ś rathinaṃ viddhi śarīra~ś rathameva tu । buddhiṃ tu sārathiṃ viddhi manaḥ pragrahameva ca ॥ 3 ॥
verses.trans: Know the Self (Atman) as the master of the chariot, and the physical body as the chariot itself. Know the intellect (Buddhi) as the charioteer, and the mind (Manas) as the reins.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: This begins the famous Chariot Analogy. Shankara points out that the Atman is completely passive—it is the owner ('Rathinam') who sits still and enjoys the journey. The body is the physical vehicle ('Ratha'). Crucially, the intellect must take the role of the driver, using the mind as a steering mechanism to control the nervous system.
commentaries.integral_yoga: Sri Aurobindo describes this as the structural map of human psychology. Most people live as if the reins (mind) are driving themselves, completely ignoring the driver (intellect) and the owner (soul). True yoga restores the organic chain of command.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: Swami Vivekananda used this blueprint to explain mental discipline. If the driver is asleep or drunk, the chariot crashes. The intellect must be sharpened and awake to handle the daily operations of human life.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: इन्द्रियाणि हयानाहुर्विषयाँस्तेषु गोचरान । आत्मेन्द्रियमनोयुक्तं भोक्तेत्याहुर्मनीषिणः ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: indriyāṇi hayānāhurviṣayā~śsteṣu gocarāna । ātmendriyamanyoktaṃ bhoktetyāhurmanīṣiṇaḥ ॥ 4 ॥
verses.trans: The senses are called the horses, and the objects of desire are the roads they travel upon. The wise declare that the Self, when unified with the body, senses, and mind, is the 'experiencer' or 'enjoyer' (Bhokta).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The five senses are naturally outward-turned, powerful animals pulling the chariot toward sensory targets ('Gochara'). Shankara stresses that the pure Atman on its own is never an enjoyer or an agent; it only appears to become the 'Bhokta' when it mistakenly identifies with the lens of the mind and the instruments of the senses.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Jiva is inherently an experiencing agent. When it associates with the physical organism, its experience is colored by material modes (Gunas). Yoga is the process of redirecting these five horses away from mundane paths and turning them toward the service of the Lord.
commentaries.universalist: The 'roads' are infinite—sights, sounds, tastes, and digital distractions. If the horses run unchecked down every road they see, the vehicle is broken apart by conflicting desires.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: यस्त्वविज्ञानवान्भवत्ययुक्तेन मनसा सदा । तस्येन्द्रियाण्यवश्यानि दुष्टाश्वा इव सारथेः ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: yastvavijñānavānbhavatyayuktena manasā sadā । tasyendriyāṇyavaśyāni duṣṭāśvā iva sāratheḥ ॥ 5 ॥
verses.trans: He who lacks discrimination and whose mind is always uncontrolled—his senses become unmanageable, like vicious, unbroken horses in the hands of an incompetent charioteer.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: 'Avijnanavan' means a person whose intellect has never been trained to distinguish between the real and the transient. Because the driver is weak, the reins (mind) remain slack, allowing the wild horses (senses) to bolt after immediate gratification, pulling the entire life into chaos.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: This describes the psychology of addiction and impulsive behavior. When a person complains, 'I know this habit is bad for me, but I can't stop doing it,' they are experiencing the state of 'Dustasva'—the horses have overpowered the driver.
commentaries.universalist: Without an inner, meditative anchor, our senses react mechanically to every external stimulus. We lose our free will and become passengers in a runaway vehicle.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: यस्तु विज्ञानवान्भवति युक्तेन मनसा सदा । तस्येन्द्रियाणि वश्यानि सदश्वा इव सारथेः ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: yastu vijñānavānbhavati yuktena manasā sadā । tasyendriyāṇi vaśyāni sadaśvā iva sāratheḥ ॥ 6 ॥
verses.trans: But he who possesses discrimination and whose mind is always controlled—his senses remain perfectly manageable, like gentle, well-trained horses guided by an expert charioteer.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The 'Vijnanavan' is the master of self-regulation. By keeping the mind ('Manas') steady and taut through focused will, the senses naturally obey the direction of the intellect, converting the energy of desire into a clean, concentrated spiritual drive.
commentaries.integral_yoga: Control does not mean violent suppression or killing the senses. It means training them. Well-trained horses perform beautifully and possess immense power; they simply move in a cohesive direction instead of pulling apart.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: A mind anchored in the continuous remembrance of the Supreme Lord gains an innate stability. The senses cease to wander because they have found a greater, more sublime focus point.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: यस्त्वविज्ञानवान्भवत्यमनस्कः सदाऽशुचिः । न स तत्पदमाप्नोति संसारं चाधिगच्छति ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: yastvavijñānavānbhavatyamanaskaḥ sadā'śuciḥ । na sa tatpadamāpnoti saṃsāraṃ cādhigacchati ॥ 7 ॥
verses.trans: He who lacks discrimination, is unmindful, and is always impure, never reaches that goal; instead, he remains trapped in the mechanical cycle of birth and death (Samsara).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: 'Amanaskah' means being scattered or devoid of introspection. Such an unexamined life is 'Asucih' (impure) because it is constantly flooded by random, reactive thoughts. Such a person cannot realize 'Tat Padam' (the absolute state of unity) because their mental mirror is too distorted.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Failing to achieve purity keeps the individual soul alienated from the divine realm. The soul is continuously cast back into material biological envelopes, experiencing the hard lessons of the evolutionary loop.
commentaries.universalist: If we do not consciously govern our internal ecosystem, we repeat our behavioral patterns indefinitely. This is the definition of Samsara—running in identical circles generation after generation.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: यस्तु विज्ञानवान्भवति समनस्कः सदा शुचिः । स तु तत्पदमाप्नोति यस्माद्भूयो न जायते ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: yastu vijñānavānbhavati samanaskaḥ sada śuciḥ । sa tu tatpadamāpnoti yasmādbhūyo na jāyate ॥ 8 ॥
verses.trans: But he who is discerning, mindful, and always pure, reaches that goal from which he is never born again.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: By maintaining 'Samanaskah' (an aligned, collected mind) and 'Sucih' (internal clarity), the seeker penetrates the illusions of individuality. Reaching the goal means realizing that one was never actually born; hence, the question of being reborn vanishes completely.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The soul reaches Vaikuntha—the infinite realm of the Divine—where it is liberated forever from the painful, involuntary gravity of karmic cycles, remaining in eternal, ecstatic communion with God.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: This verse promises definitive freedom. Spiritual realization is not a temporary emotional high; it is a permanent restructuring of human identity that breaks the cycle of psychological suffering.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: विज्ञानसारथिर्यस्तु मनःप्रग्रहवान्नरः । सोऽध्वनः पारमाप्नोति तद्विष्णोः परमं पदम् ॥ ९ ॥
verses.iast: vijñānasārathiryastu manaḥpragrahavānnaraḥ । so'dhvanaḥ pāramāpnoti tadviṣṇoḥ paramaṃ padam ॥ 9 ॥
verses.trans: The man who has a discerning intellect for his charioteer and holds the reins of the mind firmly, reaches the end of the journey—the supreme, all-pervading state of Vishnu.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara clarifies that 'Vishnu' here does not refer to a sectarian mythological deity with four arms, but to its literal etymological root: *Vyapana-shila*, meaning 'the all-pervading, omnipresent reality.' The end of the journey is the realization that your inner witness is identical with the cosmic field.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The supreme destination is explicitly the transcendental abode of Lord Narayana. The journey of the Jiva culminates in total self-surrender at the feet of the personal God, who preserves and loves all beings.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: The 'end of the journey' means the termination of human searching. The restless quest for security, love, and permanence in temporary objects finally concludes when the mind rests in its own infinite nature.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: इन्द्रियेभ्यः परा ह्यर्था अर्थेभ्यश्च परं मनः । मनसस्तु परा बुद्धिर्बुद्धेरात्मा महान्परः ॥ १० ॥
verses.iast: indriyebhyaḥ parā hyarthā arthebyhaśca paraṃ manaḥ । manasastu parā buddhirbuddherātmā mahānparaḥ ॥ 10 ॥
verses.trans: Beyond the senses are the objects of desire; beyond the objects is the mind; beyond the mind is the intellect; beyond the intellect is the Cosmic Mind (Mahat).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Yama outlines the structural hierarchy of consciousness to help Nachiketa trace his identity inward. Objects are 'beyond' the senses because senses are designed to serve them; the mind is subtler than objects; the intellect is subtler than the mind; and the 'Mahat' (the cosmic intelligence or seed of all individual minds) stands above the individual intellect.
commentaries.integral_yoga: This is a blueprint for internal sorting. To find the source, you must step backward through increasingly subtle layers of power. You move from the physical world into the psychological world, and finally into the cosmic world.
commentaries.universalist: This verse teaches a profound meditation technique: when a sensory object distracts you, trace it back to the sense organ, then back to the mind, then to the observing intellect, thereby reclaiming your focus.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: महतः परमव्यक्तमव्यक्तात्पुरुषः परः । पुरुषान्न परं किञ्चित्सा काष्ठा सा परा गतिः ॥ ११ ॥
verses.iast: mahataḥ paramavyaktamavyaktātpuruṣaḥ paraḥ । puruṣānna paraṃ kiñcitsā kāṣṭhā sā parā gatiḥ ॥ 11 ॥
verses.trans: Beyond the Cosmic Mind is the Unmanifest (Avyakta); beyond the Unmanifest is the ultimate Soul/Person (Purusha). Beyond the Purusha there is absolutely nothing. That is the final goal; that is the supreme destination.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The 'Avyakta' is the unmanifest causal state of the entire universe (Maya). Above even this potential state of matter sits the 'Purusha'—pure, unconditioned, non-dual consciousness. It is the absolute ceiling ('Kastha') of reality because consciousness requires no prior cause. It is the ultimate container.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Purusha is the Supreme Personal Godhead (Parabrahman). The 'Avyakta' is His divine material energy (Prakriti). There is no reality independent of or superior to the Supreme Lord; He is the apex of all existence.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: This is the declaration of absolute human dignity. Your innermost core is not your broken psychology or your biological body; it is the Purusha—the final, absolute reality behind the universe.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: एष सर्वेषु भूतेषु गूढोऽऽत्मा न प्रकाशते । दृश्यते त्वग्र्यया बुद्ध्या सूक्ष्मया सूक्ष्मदर्शिभिः ॥ १२ ॥
verses.iast: eṣa sarveṣu bhūteṣu gūḍho'tmā na prakāśate । dṛśyate tvagryayā buddhyā sūkṣmayā sūkṣmadarśibhiḥ ॥ 12 ॥
verses.trans: This Self, hidden deep within all beings, does not shine forth openly to the common gaze. But It can be directly perceived by subtle seers through their sharp, single-pointed, and highly refined intellects.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: If the Atman is omnipresent, why can't we see it? Shankara answers that it is 'Gudha'—veiled by the noise of sensory projections. It cannot be seen by the physical eyes, but it can be felt by 'Agryaya Buddhyā'—an intellect that has been made razor-sharp, quiet, and completely free from external fragmentation.
commentaries.integral_yoga: The soul remains an abstract concept until the mind is quieted. Just as a microscope reveals microscopic realities, a concentrated, inward-turned mind reveals the presence of pure awareness vibrating behind every thought.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: This clarifies that spirituality is a refined science of perception. It does not demand blind faith; it demands the development of a specific psychological instrument—a quiet, analytical mind capable of deep introspection.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: यच्छेद्वाङ्मनसी प्राज्ञस्तद्यच्छेज्ज्ञान आत्मनि । ज्ञानमात्मनि महति नियच्छेत्तद्यच्छेच्छान्त आत्मनि ॥ १३ ॥
verses.iast: yacchedvāṅmanasī prāñastadyacchejjñāna ātmani । jñānamātmani mahati niyacchettadyacchecchānta ātmani ॥ 13 ॥
verses.trans: Let the wise man merge his speech into the mind; let him merge that mind into the intellect; let him merge the intellect into the Cosmic Mind; and let him resolve that Cosmic Mind into the eternal Self of Peace (Shanta Atman).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: This is the precise methodology of Vedantic meditation (*Laya Chintana*). Shankara outlines a systematic process of mental dissolution: First, stop talking and withdraw vocal energy into thought ('Vak' represents all external senses). Second, resolve individual thoughts into the coordinating intellect. Third, see individual intellect as a wave of the cosmic mind. Finally, drop even that into the absolute, silent background—the Shanta Atman.
commentaries.integral_yoga: This is the path of interiorization. You travel backward through your own evolutionary history, peeling off outward expressions of identity until you touch the unmoving ground of existence.
commentaries.universalist: Meditation is presented here as an orderly rollback of attention. Instead of letting energy bleed outward into the world, you call it home, layer by layer, until nothing remains but pure, restful silence.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: उत्तिष्ठत जाग्रत प्राप्य वरान्निबोधत । क्षुरस्य धारा निशिता दुरत्यया दुर्गं पथस्तत्कवयो वदन्ति ॥ १४ ॥
verses.iast: uttiṣṭhata jāgrata prāpya varānnibodhata । kṣurasya dhārā niśitā duratyayā durgaṃ pathastatkavayo vadanti ॥ 14 ॥
verses.trans: Arise! Awake! Approach the great, illumined teachers and learn from them. Sharp as the edge of a razor, difficult to cross and hard to tread, is that path—so declare the wise.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: This is one of the ultimate battle cries of spiritual history. 'Arise' means get up from the sleep of worldly ignorance; 'Awake' means actively end your conditioning. Shankara warns that the path is 'Ksurasya Dhara' (a razor's edge) because it requires absolute mental balance—a minor slip into pride or distraction drags the seeker back into duality.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: Swami Vivekananda adopted 'Uttisthata Jagrata' as his life's motto to wake up a sleeping humanity. He interpreted it as a call for immediate, courageous self-transformation: 'Don't stop until the goal is reached!'
commentaries.universalist: The 'razor's edge' is the thin line of the present moment. Falling into memories of the past or anxieties about the future is falling off the path. It requires an extraordinary, balanced presence of mind.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: अशब्दमस्पर्शमरूपमव्ययं तथारसं नित्यमगन्धवच्च यत् । अनाद्यनन्तं महतः परं ध्रुवं निचाय्य तन्मृत्युमुखात्प्रमुच्यते ॥ १५ ॥
verses.iast: aśabdamasparśamarūpamavyayaṃ tathārasaṃ nityamagandhavacca yat । anādyanantaṃ mahataḥ paraṃ dhruvaṃ nicāyya tanmṛtyumukhātpramucyate ॥ 15 ॥
verses.trans: By realizing that Absolute Reality which is soundless, touchless, formless, imperishable, tasteless, eternal, odorless, without beginning and without end, and completely beyond the Cosmic Mind—man escapes from the terrifying jaws of Death.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara shows how death is systematically dismantled. Death can only destroy things that have physical attributes (sound, form, color, composition). Because your core consciousness is completely devoid of sensory attributes ('Asabdam', 'Asparsam'), it cannot decay, rust, or be dissolved. Recognizing this unconditioned nature pulls you directly out of the jaws of mortality.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The Supreme Lord is beyond material qualities but full of spiritual attributes. Realizing His eternal, immutable nature frees the dependent Jiva from the jurisdiction of material time and the fear of destruction.
commentaries.universalist: This verse defines true immortality. It is not the survival of your small personal ego forever; it is the realization that your true identity is that which was never subject to physical dimensions in the first place.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: नाचिकेतमुपाख्यानं मृत्युप्रोक्तं सनातनम् । उक्त्वा श्रुत्वा च मेधावी ब्रह्मलोके महीयते ॥ १६ ॥
verses.iast: nāciketamupākhyānaṃ mṛtyuproktaṃ sanātanam । uktvā śrutvā ca medhāvī brahmaloke mahīyate ॥ 16 ॥
verses.trans: The wise individual, having heard and repeated this ancient, eternal dialogue between Nachiketa and the Lord of Death, becomes magnified and glorified in the world of Brahman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: This is the *Phala-shruti* (declaration of the fruits of study). Shankara explains that 'hearing and repeating' does not mean mechanical chanting. It means absorbing the non-dual truth so thoroughly that your daily life reflects it. Becoming glorified in the world of Brahman means realizing that you *are* the absolute field.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Contemplating this sacred exchange purifies the mind of egoism. It elevates the soul’s consciousness, allowing it to transcend mundane attachments and prepare for final entry into the spiritual realm of God.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: This dialogue is described as 'Sanatanamp' (eternal) because the questions Nachiketa asks are the fundamental questions of every human being, regardless of century, culture, or geographic location.

verses.sec: Chapter I · Vallī 3 — The Chariot Analogy
verses.dev: य इमं परमं गुह्यं श्रावयेद् ब्रह्मसंसदि । प्रयतश्चाद्धकाले वा तदानन्त्याय कल्पते तदानन्त्याय कल्पते इति ॥ १७ ॥
verses.iast: ya imaṃ paramaṃ guhyaṃ śrāvayed brahmasaṃsadi । prayataścāddhakāle vā tadānantyāya kalpate tadānantyāya kalpate iti ॥ 17 ॥
verses.trans: Whoever, with deep devotion and a purified mind, recites this supreme secret in an assembly of seekers, or performs it during rituals of remembrance, attains infinite rewards. Indeed, he attains infinity!

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The repetition of the final words ('tadānantyāya kalpate') marks the definitive conclusion of the entire First Chapter of the Upanishad. Shankara notes that sharing this knowledge with a contemplative assembly creates a powerful collective resonance that accelerates liberation. The reward is 'Anantya'—absolute, unconditioned infinity.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Sharing these ultimate truths with sincere lovers of God is an act of supreme spiritual service. It opens the floodgates of divine grace for both the speaker and the listener, anchoring them in the eternal bliss of devotion.
commentaries.universalist: The text closes by emphasizing the sacredness of communication. When human beings gather to discuss reality rather than superficialities, they pierce through the veil of localized ego and step into the infinite.

verses.sec: Chapter II · Vallī 1 — The Inward Turn
verses.dev: पराञ्चि खानि व्यतृणत् स्वयम्भूस्तस्मात्पराङ्पश्यति नान्तरात्मन् । कश्चिद्धीरः प्रत्यगात्मानमैक्षदावृत्तचक्षुरमृतत्वमिच्छन् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: parāñci khāni vyatṛṇat svayambhūstasmātparāṅpaśyati nāntarātman । kaściddhīraḥ pratyagātmānamaikṣadāvṛttacakṣuramṛtatvamicchan ॥ 1 ॥
verses.trans: The Self-Existent Creator pierced the sense-openings outward; therefore, one naturally looks outward, and not inward into oneself. But a certain wise hero, desiring immortality, turned his vision completely inward and beheld the indwelling Self (Pratyagatman).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that 'Vyatrinat' means injured or cursed. By designing the sense organs (eyes, ears, mind) to look exclusively outward, the Creator structurally hidden the true Subject. The common gaze is addicted to objects. A rare, courageous person ('Dhira') acts as a cosmic rebel—practicing 'Avritti-Chakshu' (reversing the flow of attention) to witness the pure awareness behind the eyes.
commentaries.integral_yoga: Sri Aurobindo notes that our biological evolution forces an outward focus for survival in the material matrix. True yoga requires a radical psychic inversion—overcoming the momentum of nature to establish a subjective center of gravity.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: This verse explains the root cause of human distraction. We are biologically hardwired to search for satisfaction outside of ourselves. The breakthrough of spirituality is realizing that the ultimate peace we look for is actually the watcher of the search.

verses.sec: Chapter II · Vallī 1 — The Inward Turn
verses.dev: पराचः कामाननुयन्ति बालास्ते मृत्योर्यन्ति विततस्य पाशम् । अथ धीरा अमृतत्वं विदित्वा ध्रुवमध्रुवेष्विह न प्रार्थयन्ते ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: parācaḥ kāmānanuyanti bālāste mṛtyoryanti vitatasya pāśam । atha dhīrā amṛtatvaṃ viditā dhruvamadhruveṣviha na prārthayante ॥ 2 ॥
verses.trans: The childish and spiritually immature run after external pleasures; they walk straight into the widespread snare of Death. But the wise, clearly knowing what true immortality is, never seek for the permanent amidst the changing things of this world.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: 'Balah' refers to people who possess chronological age but lack metaphysical maturity. They treat the world as a permanent playground, becoming helplessly caught in Yama’s web of cause, effect, and rebirth. The wise stop projecting their demands for absolute happiness onto relative, changing ('Adhruva') objects.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Material desires act as heavy anchors that weigh the soul down into lower bodily realms. Realizing that the only stable, permanent destination is the Lord, the devotee withdraws their expectations from the world and fixes them entirely on God.
commentaries.universalist: This points out a logical contradiction in standard human behavior: we search for an infinite sense of satisfaction inside finite relationships, careers, and possessions. Wisdom begins when we stop asking temporary things to yield eternal rewards.

verses.sec: Chapter II · Vallī 1 — The Inward Turn
verses.dev: येन रूपं रसं गन्धं शब्दान् स्पर्शाँश्च मैथुनान् । एतेनैव विजानाति किमत्र परिशिष्यते । एतद्वै तत् ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: yena rūpaṃ rasaṃ gandhaṃ śabdān sparśā~śca maithunān । etenaiva vijānāti kimatra pariśiṣyate । etadvai tat ॥ 3 ॥
verses.trans: That by which one experiences form, taste, smell, sound, touch, and the pleasures of intimacy—by that Presence alone does one know everything. What else remains here independent of It? This is, in truth, That which thou hast asked for.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara presents a devastating epistemological point. The world cannot prove itself; it requires your consciousness to validate its existence. You see a color *because* of awareness; you feel a touch *because* of awareness. The light of the Self is the ultimate laboratory. When you strip away everything perceived, what is left? The Perceiver. 'Etad vai tat'—This is exactly what Nachiketa asked to know.
commentaries.modern_vedanta: Swami Sarvapriyananda uses this verse to demonstrate that the Atman is not a mystical object to be found in a cave; it is the very consciousness reading these words right now. It is the undeniable background of every single experience.
commentaries.universalist: We often mistake the contents of our awareness for reality. This verse pulls our attention away from *what* we are experiencing and focuses it squarely on the fact *that* we are conscious.

verses.sec: Chapter II · Vallī 1 — The Inward Turn
verses.dev: स्वप्नान्तं जागरितान्तं चोभौ येनानुपश्यति । महान्तं विभुमात्मानं मत्वा धीरो न शोचति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: svapnāntaṃ jāgaritāntaṃ cobhau yenānupaśyati । mahāntaṃ vibhumātmānaṃ matvā dhīro na śocati ॥ 4 ॥
verses.trans: Realizing that great, all-pervading Self by which one perceives both the dream state and the waking state, the wise individual leaves all sorrow behind.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: This outlines the foundational Vedantic method of the Three States of Consciousness (*Avastha-Traya*). The characters in your dream vanish when you wake up; the environments of your waking life vanish when you sleep. But *You* remain constant as the witness of both setups. Recognizing yourself as the unchanging screen rather than the changing movie removes the capacity for grief.
